title.english: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Traditional Fishing Practices
title.teReoMāori: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku: Ngā Tikanga Hī Ika o Neherā

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the history of Ngāti Kahungunu and the Mangakōtukutuku Stream that flows through our rohe. Students will explore traditional fishing methods (hī ika) that were practiced along this awa by their tūpuna, connecting language learning with the specific environmental features and cultural practices of our local area. The stream's name itself tells a story - 'manga' meaning stream, and 'kōtukutuku' referring to the native tree fuchsia that grows along its banks, whose berries were used as bait.
learningObjectives: Identify and name at least 5 traditional fishing tools and methods used in Ngāti Kahungunu in te reo Māori
Construct simple sentences in te reo Māori describing how tūpuna used the Mangakōtukutuku Stream for sustainable fishing
Create a digital story in te reo Māori explaining the cultural significance of one traditional fishing practice
Participate in a wānanga with local kaumātua using appropriate te reo Māori greetings and questions

activities.title: He Hīkoi Hītori - Historical Walk Along the Awa
activities.description: Students will walk along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with kaumātua, identifying traditional fishing spots and learning the Māori names for local flora/fauna used in fishing practices. They will record vocabulary and observations in their pukapuka.

activities.title: Ngā Taputapu Hī Ika - Traditional Tool Workshop
activities.description: Working with local experts, students will handle and learn to name traditional fishing tools (pā kahawai, hinaki, pātia, etc.) while practicing sentence structures describing their use and purpose in te reo Māori.

activities.title: He Kōrero mō Te Awa - Digital Storytelling
activities.description: Using iPads, students will create short digital stories in te reo Māori explaining the cultural significance of one fishing method, incorporating images of the actual Mangakōtukutuku Stream and traditional practices.

activities.title: Te Wānanga ki te Kaumātua - Elder Knowledge Session
activities.description: Students will prepare and ask questions in te reo Māori to local kaumātua about traditional fishing practices, recording the responses and creating a shared class knowledge bank.

activities.title: He Mahi Toi - Artistic Representation
activities.description: Students will create artwork depicting traditional fishing scenes along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, labeling all elements in te reo Māori and writing descriptive captions using newly acquired vocabulary.

resources: Kaumātua Hōri Waititi (local Ngāti Kahungunu elder and fishing knowledge holder)
'Ngā Mahi a te Whānau' by Hirini Melbourne (specific chapter on traditional fishing)
Te Papa Tongarewa online collection of traditional Māori fishing tools
Mangakōtukutuku Stream ecological survey maps from Hawke's Bay Regional Council

tasks: Vocabulary quiz on traditional fishing terms in te reo Māori
Oral presentation describing a traditional fishing method using full sentences
Peer assessment of digital stories using te reo Māori criteria
Self-reflection on cultural learning in te reo Māori journal

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori language patterns and vocabulary to communicate about immediate and familiar environment
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 4: Recognize that Māori language is used for different purposes and in different situations
Social Sciences: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
Technology: Use a range of media to present information and tell stories
